review.note: AI-drafted first pass - SME review required before production

sources.title: History Grade 10 - Topic 1 The Ming Dynasty
History Grade 10 - Topic 1 The Mughal Empire

slides.heading: The World Around 1600
slides.lead: Four rich, powerful and very different societies - Ming China, Songhai, Mughal India and Europe - on the eve of a connected, global age.

image.alt: A stylised world map with four glowing regions marked across Asia, West Africa and Europe.

slides.notes: Open by challenging the common assumption that Europe 'ruled' the world in 1600. In fact it did not. Around 1600 the wealthiest, most populous and most administratively advanced states were in Asia and Africa. Set up the lesson's central task: to compare four major societies fairly, and to ask why the balance of power would shift over the following centuries. Define 'around 1600' as roughly 1450-1650.

slides.heading: Why study the world of 1600?
slides.lead: 1600 is a hinge point - just before Europe's overseas expansion reshaped the globe.

blocks.text: Studying several societies at the same moment lets us **compare** them fairly, instead of telling only Europe's story. Around 1600 the world's centres of wealth, learning and power lay mostly **outside** Europe.

items: It sets a baseline before European colonial expansion accelerated.
It corrects a Eurocentric view by showing sophisticated African and Asian states.
It helps explain the roots of later conquest, trade and slavery that shaped South Africa.

slides.notes: Stress the analytical purpose (FET level): comparison, perspective and causation. Warn learners against a 'Europe was always ahead' narrative - it is historically wrong for 1600. Link forward: the Dutch East India Company (VOC), founded in 1602, would later establish a refreshment station at the Cape in 1652, which is why this global picture matters for South African history.

slides.heading: The big picture, c.1600
slides.lead: Each society was organised differently, yet each was wealthy and highly developed.

blocks.heading: Ming China
blocks.text: The world's largest, richest state - a vast centralised empire run by a trained civil service.

blocks.heading: Songhai
blocks.text: West Africa's greatest empire of the 1500s, built on gold, salt and Islamic scholarship.

blocks.heading: Mughal India
blocks.text: A wealthy, diverse empire famous for textiles, art and (under Akbar) religious tolerance.

blocks.heading: Europe
blocks.text: Not one state but many rival kingdoms - fragmented, competitive and turning to the sea.

slides.notes: Use these four cards as the map for the whole lesson - the next four slides take each in turn. Ask learners to predict, from these one-liners alone, which society they think was most powerful in 1600 and why. Most will guess Europe; the lesson will complicate that guess.

slides.heading: Ming China (1368-1644)
slides.lead: Around 1600 China was the most populous and probably the wealthiest society on Earth.

blocks.text: The **Ming dynasty** ruled a unified empire of well over 100 million people. Government was run by a professional bureaucracy chosen through the demanding **civil-service examinations**, which tested knowledge of Confucian texts. The emperor ruled from the **Forbidden City** in Beijing.

items: China exported silk, tea and fine porcelain, drawing in huge amounts of American and Japanese silver.
Earlier Ming treasure fleets under Admiral Zheng He (1405-1433) had sailed as far as East Africa.
By 1600 the state had largely turned inward, restricting overseas voyages and rebuilding the Great Wall.

image.alt: A symmetrical Chinese palace gate with tiered roofs, representing the Forbidden City.
image.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 220 160' role='img'><rect width='220' height='160' fill='#f4efe6'/><rect x='40' y='96' width='140' height='44' fill='#8a1f1f'/><rect x='40' y='86' width='140' height='12' fill='#5c1414'/><path d='M30 86 L110 56 L190 86 Z' fill='#c8892f'/><path d='M52 60 L110 36 L168 60 Z' fill='#e0a33e'/><rect x='100' y='112' width='20' height='28' fill='#3a1010'/><rect x='60' y='112' width='14' height='20' fill='#f4efe6'/><rect x='146' y='112' width='14' height='20' fill='#f4efe6'/></svg>

slides.notes: Emphasise scale and sophistication: a merit-based bureaucracy centuries before most of Europe had one, and an economy so large it absorbed much of the world's silver. Explain the significance of the withdrawal of the treasure fleets - a choice, not an inability - which meant China was not seeking overseas empires just as Europeans began to. [SME] Verify population and silver-trade figures against a current textbook before production.

slides.heading: The Songhai Empire
slides.lead: The largest empire in West African history, at its height across the 1400s and 1500s.

blocks.text: Centred on the city of **Gao** on the Niger River, Songhai grew powerful under **Sunni Ali** and especially **Askia Muhammad** (ruled 1493-1528). Its wealth came from controlling the **trans-Saharan trade** in gold and salt, and it was a Muslim state with a strong administration and army.

items: Timbuktu and Djenne were famous centres of Islamic learning, with universities and libraries of manuscripts.
Traders exchanged West African gold for salt, cloth, books and horses from North Africa.
In 1591 a Moroccan army with firearms defeated Songhai at the Battle of Tondibi, and the empire broke apart.

image.alt: A mud-brick mosque with wooden support beams and a tall minaret, like the Great Mosque of Djenne.
image.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 220 160' role='img'><rect width='220' height='160' fill='#f4efe6'/><rect y='128' width='220' height='16' fill='#c8b89a'/><rect x='40' y='70' width='120' height='58' fill='#c9852f'/><rect x='96' y='40' width='24' height='88' fill='#c9852f'/><rect x='102' y='24' width='12' height='16' fill='#c9852f'/><circle cx='108' cy='22' r='4' fill='#e0a33e'/><rect x='56' y='58' width='10' height='70' fill='#b5771f'/><rect x='134' y='58' width='10' height='70' fill='#b5771f'/><g fill='#7a4d18'><rect x='48' y='84' width='10' height='4'/><rect x='142' y='84' width='10' height='4'/><rect x='48' y='100' width='10' height='4'/><rect x='142' y='100' width='10' height='4'/></g></svg>

slides.notes: This slide carries an important nuance: strictly, Songhai collapsed in 1591, just before 1600. CAPS still studies it as a major society 'around 1600' because it dominated West Africa for the preceding century. Be explicit with learners about this timing. Use Timbuktu's manuscripts to counter the myth that pre-colonial Africa had no written scholarship. [SME] Confirm Askia Muhammad's dates and the 1591 date/Battle of Tondibi against SAHO's Songhai article.

slides.heading: Mughal India
slides.lead: Around 1600 the Mughal Empire under Akbar was one of the world's wealthiest and most cultured states.

blocks.text: The **Mughal Empire** (founded 1526) controlled most of the Indian subcontinent. Its greatest ruler, **Akbar** (ruled 1556-1605), built an efficient tax and administrative system and promoted a policy of **religious tolerance** (sulh-i-kul, 'peace with all') among Hindus, Muslims and others.

items: India was the world's leading producer of fine cotton textiles, traded across Asia, Africa and Europe.
Mughal courts produced spectacular art, architecture (later the Taj Mahal) and literature.
European trading companies came to India as junior partners seeking access to its goods.

image.alt: A domed palace with pointed arches and slender towers, in the Mughal architectural style.
image.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 220 160' role='img'><rect width='220' height='160' fill='#f4efe6'/><rect y='128' width='220' height='16' fill='#c8b89a'/><rect x='58' y='78' width='104' height='50' fill='#e4ddd0'/><path d='M78 78 a32 34 0 0 1 64 0 Z' fill='#dcd3c2'/><path d='M96 40 q14 -22 28 0 q6 20 -14 20 q-20 0 -14 -20 Z' fill='#dcd3c2'/><circle cx='110' cy='30' r='4' fill='#c8892f'/><rect x='44' y='70' width='10' height='58' fill='#e4ddd0'/><rect x='166' y='70' width='10' height='58' fill='#e4ddd0'/><path d='M100 104 a10 12 0 0 1 20 0 Z' fill='#8a6d3a'/></svg>

slides.notes: Highlight two comparison points: Mughal wealth (India's textile industry was globally dominant) and Akbar's deliberate tolerance, which is a striking contrast with Europe's religious wars of the same period. Note the power relationship: European merchants arrived as supplicants, not conquerors - the reverse of the later colonial picture. This reframes the direction of 1600s power.

slides.heading: European Societies
slides.lead: Not a single empire but many competing states - fragmented, restless and turning outward.

blocks.text: Europe around 1600 was divided into rival kingdoms and city-states, unsettled by the **Renaissance**, the **Reformation** and its religious wars. Smaller and poorer than Ming China or Mughal India, it was nonetheless developing new tools that would matter enormously: the **printing press**, gunpowder weapons, ocean-going ships and joint-stock companies.

items: Portugal and Spain had already opened sea routes to the Americas, Africa and Asia.
The English (1600) and Dutch (VOC, 1602) East India Companies were founded to profit from Asian trade.
Competition between states drove exploration, warfare and the beginnings of overseas colonies.

image.alt: A three-masted sailing ship with billowing sails on stylised waves.
image.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 220 160' role='img'><rect width='220' height='160' fill='#cfe0e8'/><path d='M0 120 q30 -8 55 0 t55 0 t55 0 t55 0 v40 H0 Z' fill='#7fa7bb'/><path d='M60 118 h100 l-14 -20 H74 Z' fill='#6b3f1d'/><rect x='108' y='40' width='4' height='60' fill='#4a2c14'/><path d='M112 46 q34 6 30 40 l-30 0 Z' fill='#f4efe6'/><path d='M108 46 q-34 6 -30 40 l30 0 Z' fill='#e6dccb'/><path d='M110 40 l14 -6 l-14 -4 Z' fill='#8a1f1f'/></svg>

slides.notes: Correct the ranking: in 1600 Europe was on the margins, not the centre. Its advantage was not size or wealth but a mix of competition, sea power, finance (joint-stock companies) and military technology, plus access to American silver and land. Introduce the VOC here so learners hold the thread to the Cape in 1652. Frame European expansion neutrally now; the ethics come on the later slides.

slides.heading: Comparing the four societies
slides.lead: Look for patterns of similarity and difference across government, wealth and outlook.

items: Large populations and productive farming.
Long-distance trade networks and cities.
Organised states with rulers, taxes and armies.
Rich religious, scholarly and artistic life.
China and the Mughals: single vast empires; Europe: many rival states.
Songhai and Mughals: Islamic and multi-faith rule; Europe: divided by the Reformation.
China turned inward; Europe turned outward to the oceans.
Europe developed joint-stock companies to finance overseas ventures.

slides.notes: This is the analytical heart of the lesson. Model comparative thinking: choose a theme (e.g. government, religion, or attitude to overseas expansion) and trace it across all four societies. Push learners past listing facts towards explaining significance - especially the contrast between China turning inward and Europe turning outward, which helps explain later shifts in global power.

slides.heading: Trade tied the world together
slides.lead: By 1600 goods, silver, ideas and people moved between all these societies.

blocks.text: These societies were not isolated. The **trans-Saharan** routes linked West Africa to the Mediterranean; the **Indian Ocean** network connected East Africa, Arabia, India and China; and new **Atlantic** routes joined Europe, Africa and the Americas. American silver flowed east to buy Chinese silk and porcelain.

items: Gold, salt, ivory, textiles, spices and porcelain crossed continents.
The Atlantic routes also carried the growing, brutal trade in enslaved Africans.
This 'first global age' set the stage for European colonial expansion.

image.alt: A simple diagram of trade arrows linking West Africa, Europe, India and China.
image.data: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' viewBox='0 0 260 150' role='img'><rect width='260' height='150' fill='#eef3f6'/><g font-family='sans-serif' font-size='11' fill='#333'><circle cx='50' cy='95' r='16' fill='#c9b98f'/><text x='50' y='125' text-anchor='middle'>W. Africa</text><circle cx='110' cy='40' r='16' fill='#b7c8a8'/><text x='110' y='20' text-anchor='middle'>Europe</text><circle cx='170' cy='95' r='16' fill='#c9852f'/><text x='170' y='125' text-anchor='middle'>India</text><circle cx='225' cy='45' r='16' fill='#8a1f1f'/><text x='225' y='26' text-anchor='middle'>China</text></g><g stroke='#8a1f1f' stroke-width='2' fill='none'><path d='M64 88 L98 50'/><path d='M124 48 L156 86'/><path d='M184 86 L212 56'/></g></svg>

slides.notes: Reinforce interconnection: 1600 is often called the start of the 'first global age' because for the first time all the major world regions were linked by regular trade. Introduce the transatlantic slave trade honestly but age-appropriately here - name it as a system of forced labour and human suffering that grew alongside this trade, without graphic detail. It returns on the ethics slide.

slides.heading: Key moments, c.1450-1652

blocks.alt: A timeline running from the mid-1400s to 1652 marking key events in China, Africa, India and Europe.

events.label: Askia Muhammad begins Songhai's golden age
Moroccan invasion ends the Songhai Empire
English and Dutch (VOC) East India Companies founded
VOC starts a refreshment station at the Cape

slides.notes: Use the timeline to fix the sequence and to make the South African link explicit at the end: the same European commercial expansion visible in 1600 reached the Cape in 1652. Ask learners to notice how close together 1591 (Songhai's fall) and 1600-1602 (the trading companies) are - a symbolic handover of momentum.

slides.heading: Whose story do we tell?
slides.lead: The evidence of 1600 challenges the idea that Europe was always the world's leader.

blocks.text: For a long time history was written mainly from a **European viewpoint**, treating other societies as 'backward'. The world of 1600 shows the opposite: China, Songhai and Mughal India were as advanced as - often more advanced than - Europe. Good historians weigh **different perspectives** and question their sources.

items: Ask who produced a source, and whose voice is missing.
African and Asian achievements are central, not a footnote, to world history.
Understanding 1600 fairly helps us understand how power later shifted - and why.

slides.notes: This slide builds the CAPS skill of working with perspective and bias. Encourage learners to name the danger of a single story. Tie it to their own context: much of the history of colonialism in South Africa was long told from the settler viewpoint. The corrective is not to flip into a new myth but to weigh evidence carefully.

slides.heading: Expansion, conquest and slavery
slides.lead: The connected world of 1600 also opened the door to conquest and the trade in enslaved people.

blocks.text: European overseas expansion brought new trade and contact, but it also brought **colonial conquest** and a growing **transatlantic slave trade** that forced millions of Africans into bondage. We study this honestly, remembering that these were real people, not just statistics.
History is not only about the powerful; it must also recover the experiences of those who were conquered, traded and silenced.

items: Expansion had winners and victims - both belong in the record.
The slave trade's legacy still shapes societies, including South Africa, today.

slides.notes: Approach this slide with care and dignity. The goal is honesty without sensationalism: name colonial violence and the slave trade clearly, acknowledge the humanity of the enslaved and conquered, and avoid graphic imagery. Invite reflective (not distressing) discussion. This anchors the ethical dimension CAPS expects and connects world history to South Africa's own past.

slides.heading: Review: the world around 1600
slides.lead: Around 1600 the world's great powers were spread across Asia and Africa; Europe's rise still lay ahead.

blocks.text: Ming China, Songhai and Mughal India were wealthy, well-governed and culturally rich, while a fragmented Europe was only beginning to reach outward. Trade tied these worlds together - and that connection would soon reshape the balance of global power, eventually reaching the Cape.

blocks.question: Compare Ming China and Europe around 1600 in terms of political organisation and attitude to overseas expansion.
Explain how trade connected West Africa, India and China to the wider world by 1600. Refer to at least two trade networks.
"In 1600, Europe was not yet the most powerful part of the world." Using evidence from at least two societies, do you agree? Explain your answer.

slides.notes: Close by returning to the opening question: who was 'ahead' in 1600? The evidence says not Europe. Use the three questions for written assessment or debate; the mark weightings mirror CAPS source-based and extended-writing tasks. The 10-mark question is an extended-writing prompt requiring a clear line of argument and evidence. Remind learners this topic sets up Grade 10's later study of colonial expansion in South Africa.
